cataloging_subject.label: The Lyfe of Ipomydon
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English B version of Ipomydon: one 2,346-line verse romance independently translated, condensed, and adapted from Hue de Rotelande's late-twelfth-century Anglo-Norman Ipomedon. The work is distinct from the tail-rhyme A version, prose C version, its unique Harley MS 2252 copy, the two de Worde print states, Ikegami's 1983 and 1985 editions, the OTA/U-M encodings, later scholarship, and repository XML. The ten DIV1/LG containers in the XML are supplied navigation, not ten constituent works or authorial sections.

catalog_summary.title: The Lyfe of Ipomydon
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator-adapter
catalog_summary.date: Probably the second half of the fifteenth century

notes: Established title of the B version and the immediate edition; spelling preserves the edition's Lyfe and Ipomydon forms.
The version label distinguishes this short-couplet text from the tail-rhyme A and prose C Middle English renditions.
Normalized from the repository XML's bracketed-expansion representation and not counted as a 2,347th verse line.

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English translator-adapter
notes: No personal name is securely associated with the B version. The record uses a collective cataloging placeholder without assuming multiple people.

agents.name: Tadahiro Ikegami
notes: Editor and transcriber of the immediate 1983 Seijo source edition.

scope.description: Independent Middle English rendering of Hue de Rotelande's Anglo-Norman Ipomedon

assertion.notes: Both modern sources distinguish the B text from Hue's source work and from the other two Middle English renditions.

notes: Hue de Rotelande is not assigned target-work authorship.

scope.description: Substantial condensation and reshaping into a 2,346-line Middle English popular romance

notes: Translator and adapter describe two functions of the same unknown responsibility, not two known people.

scope.description: Immediate scholarly source edition, The Lyfe of Ipomydon, volume I (1983)
Conservative edited transcription from Harley MS 2252, ff. 54r-84r

notes: This edition-level role does not make Ikegami an author of the medieval romance.

scope.description: Comparison of manuscript readings and corrections with earlier Kölbing and Weber editions

date_statements.display: Probably the second half of the fifteenth century

assertion.notes: Ikegami argues for post-1400 and probably the latter half of the fifteenth century; Mayer's phonological analysis supports the same probable half-century.

notes: This dates the anonymous B work, not Hue's twelfth-century source, the early-sixteenth-century composite volume, or the 1983 edition.

date_statements.display: Second half of the fifteenth century in British Library Harley MS 2252
notes: Harley 2252 is the unique manuscript copy of the B version. This does not imply that its surviving copy is the author's holograph.

date_statements.display: By approximately 1520-1524 under the current Morgan dating; exact print order and dates remain disputed

assertion.notes: The Morgan record dates PML 20896.2 approximately 1520-1524 and calls it possibly earliest; traditional records date the Bagford state c. 1522, but scholarly accounts disagree about ordering.

notes: No single source-witness binding is attached because the earliest surviving state remains contested.

places.label: North-East Midlands, England
notes: A linguistic region rather than a securely documented place of composition. Ikegami discusses a more specific North Rutland / north-east Leicestershire / adjacent south-west Lincolnshire probability, but the broader label is retained.
The English language, manuscript origin, and early transmission support broad cultural origin; this is not a city-level composition claim.

assertion.notes: The localization is a scholarly inference from linguistic evidence, not a colophon statement.

notes: Do not transfer the British Library's broad manuscript origin England or Colyns's later London ownership into a more precise authorial place.

scope.description: Early printed transmission by Wynkyn de Worde

notes: London is the de Worde imprint place and Colyns transmission context, not an asserted place of medieval composition.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English introductions and catalog metadata belong to edition or digital layers and do not make the medieval work bilingual.

form_statements.description: A 2,346-line narrative romance in rhyming couplets, followed by a brief explicit. The explicit and supplied navigation do not make the work mixed-form.

genre_statements.label: Middle English romance
notes: Mayer describes B as a popular romance; the normalized corpus genre uses the established broader label Middle English romance.

genre_statements.label: Chivalric romance
notes: Knightly service, tournament victories, combat, concealed identity, honor, inheritance, and marriage structure the narrative.

subject_statements.label: Chivalric service, combat, hunting, and tournament
Kinship, inheritance, and reunion
notes: The hero's royal inheritance and recognition of his half-brother Campanus/Capanius converge with the final marriage and settlement.

subject_statements.label: Manuscript-to-print transmission
notes: Harley MS 2252 was prepared for early print use; the exact order and directness of the two de Worde states remain disputed.

summaries.value: Ipomydon, heir to Apulia, hears of the beauty and prowess of the lady who rules Calabria and enters her service without revealing his royal identity. He excels in hunting and wins a great tournament while appearing on successive days in white, red, and black, yet conceals his victories and departs before accepting marriage. When the lady is later threatened by Duke Geron, Ipomydon returns disguised as a fool, secures the right to answer her appeal for aid, defends her messenger from three attackers, defeats Geron, and is temporarily mistaken for him. A combat leads to recognition of his long-lost half-brother Campanus; Ipomydon's disguises and earlier deeds are revealed, he reunites with the lady, and the romance closes with marriage, kingship, rewards, and a prayer for salvation.

This record catalogs the anonymous Middle English B work, not Hue de Rotelande's Anglo-Norman source, another Middle English version, a manuscript or early-print carrier, Ikegami's editions, the OTA/U-M encodings, or the repository file. The XML's ten large DIV1/LG units are supplied navigation. The verse extent is 2,346 lines; XML L 2347 is the explicit and is excluded from the verse-line count.

content_parts.label: The Lyfe of Ipomydon
content_parts.summary: Complete 2,346-line B-version romance from the appeal to listeners through Ipomydon's upbringing, disguised service and tournaments, departure and return as a fool, rescue of the lady, fraternal recognition, revelation, marriage, settlement, and terminal prayer.
notes: The ten XML LG containers do not correspond to stanzas or evidenced authorial divisions; verse_groups is therefore unknown/null rather than ten.
The prose-like explicit is paratext and does not make the work mixed-form.

extent.description: One complete 2,346-line narrative poem in rhyming couplets, followed by an explicit. The repository XML has 10 supplied DIV1 and 10 supplied LG navigation containers, 2,347 L elements, 31 folio milestones, and 83 page milestones. XML line 2347 is the explicit; those encoding structures do not make the work ten sections, 2,347 verse lines, or mixed prose and verse.

related_works.title: Ipomedon
notes: Hue de Rotelande's 10,580-line late-twelfth-century Anglo-Norman romance is the source work from which B independently translates, condenses, and adapts. No surviving physical Anglo-Norman exemplar is identified as the anonymous English adapter's copy.

related_works.title: Ipomadon (A version)
notes: A distinct Middle English tail-rhyme rendition preserved in Manchester, Chetham's Library MS 8009, ff. 191-335; it is not an alternate witness of the B text.

related_works.title: Ipomedon (C version)
notes: A distinct incomplete Middle English prose rendition preserved in Longleat House MS 257, ff. 90r-106v. Mayer edits it beside B for comparison, but it is not a prose section of the cataloged work.

Its current manuscript date and print holdings/extents conflict with stronger direct evidence and are not adopted uncritically.

evidence.summary: Linked direct scholarship for independent B/C derivation from Hue, B's anonymous 2,346-line popular-romance identity, North-East Midlands linguistic and second-half-fifteenth-century conclusions, plot/themes, distinct version boundaries, and later edition locators.

evidence.citation: Ipomydon source-lineage manifest, de Worde print and Harley setting-copy evidence.

open_questions.question: Can the B version's composition date and place be narrowed beyond a probable second half of the fifteenth century and North-East Midlands linguistic region?
next_steps: Review post-Ikegami dialect and localization scholarship using current corpus methods.
Keep dialect localization distinct from manuscript-copy origin, London ownership, and London printing.

open_questions.question: Which physical Anglo-Norman exemplar or textual state did the anonymous B translator-adapter use?
next_steps: Consult detailed comparative studies of Hue's Ipomedon and the A, B, and C Middle English renditions.
Do not substitute a surviving modern edition or representative Anglo-Norman witness for the unidentified historical exemplar.

open_questions.question: Which de Worde state is the earliest surviving publication, and what date should be preferred for each?
next_steps: Reconcile current Morgan, ESTC/USTC, Sánchez-Martí, Ikegami, and Bagford evidence.
Retain the broad current Morgan approximately 1520-1524 range as tentative discovery metadata without asserting a print-to-print direction.

notes: Hue de Rotelande's name in legacy U-M/OTA and Morgan creator fields belongs to the source-work layer. The target B version remains anonymous.
Robert Copland's surviving envoi supports a printer or print-shop role in one early edition, not authorship or adaptation of the medieval B text.
The current British Library catalog's DIMEV 3462-1 is retained as the active occurrence identifier; historical IMEV/NIMEV 2142 remains separately recorded for older scholarship.
Rights and access differ across the medieval work, manuscript, early prints, Ikegami editions, electronic encodings, and page images; see the linked lineage manifest.
